---
title: "Chapter 04 — 🧹 Data Preprocessing & Feature Engineering"
page-layout: full
toc: true
toc-location: left
toc-title: "In this chapter"
toc-depth: 2
toc-expand: true
---

[📖 All chapters](../ai-ml-encyclopedia.html)  &nbsp;|&nbsp;  [← 03 · 🔄 The Machine Learning Process](ch03.html)  &nbsp;|&nbsp;  [05 · 📈 Linear & Logistic Models →](ch05.html)

<details class="ig-jump">
<summary>📚 Jump to any chapter</summary>
<p class="ig-era">🌐 Foundations</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch01.html">01 &middot; 🌐 The Landscape of AI</a></li>
<li><a href="ch02.html">02 &middot; 🧮 Mathematics for Machine Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch03.html">03 &middot; 🔄 The Machine Learning Process</a></li>
<li><a href="ch04.html">04 &middot; 🧹 Data Preprocessing & Feature Engineering</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🧩 Classical Machine Learning</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch05.html">05 &middot; 📈 Linear & Logistic Models</a></li>
<li><a href="ch06.html">06 &middot; 📍 Instance-Based & Probabilistic Classifiers</a></li>
<li><a href="ch07.html">07 &middot; 🛡️ Support Vector Machines & Kernels</a></li>
<li><a href="ch08.html">08 &middot; 🌳 Decision Trees & Ensemble Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch09.html">09 &middot; 🔮 Clustering & Unsupervised Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch10.html">10 &middot; 🗜️ Dimensionality Reduction & Manifold Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch11.html">11 &middot; 🎯 Model Evaluation, Validation & Selection</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🎲 Probabilistic & Statistical ML</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch12.html">12 &middot; 🎲 Bayesian Machine Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch13.html">13 &middot; 🕸️ Probabilistic Graphical Models</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🧠 Deep Learning</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch14.html">14 &middot; 🧠 Neural Network Fundamentals</a></li>
<li><a href="ch15.html">15 &middot; ⚙️ Training Deep Neural Networks</a></li>
<li><a href="ch16.html">16 &middot; 🖼️ Convolutional Neural Networks</a></li>
<li><a href="ch17.html">17 &middot; 🔁 Recurrent & Sequence Models</a></li>
<li><a href="ch18.html">18 &middot; ⚡ Attention & Transformers</a></li>
<li><a href="ch19.html">19 &middot; 🪞 Autoencoders & Self-Supervised Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch20.html">20 &middot; 🎨 Generative Models: GANs, VAEs & Flows</a></li>
<li><a href="ch21.html">21 &middot; 🌫️ Diffusion Models</a></li>
<li><a href="ch22.html">22 &middot; 🔗 Graph Neural Networks</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🕹️ Reinforcement Learning</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch23.html">23 &middot; 🕹️ Reinforcement Learning Foundations</a></li>
<li><a href="ch24.html">24 &middot; 🎰 Model-Free Reinforcement Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch25.html">25 &middot; 🤖 Deep Reinforcement Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch26.html">26 &middot; ♟️ Multi-Agent RL & Game Theory</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🗣️ Language, Vision & Modalities</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch27.html">27 &middot; 👁️ Computer Vision</a></li>
<li><a href="ch28.html">28 &middot; 💬 Natural Language Processing</a></li>
<li><a href="ch29.html">29 &middot; 🔊 Speech & Audio Processing</a></li>
<li><a href="ch30.html">30 &middot; 📚 Large Language Models</a></li>
<li><a href="ch31.html">31 &middot; 🎚️ Prompting, Fine-Tuning & Alignment</a></li>
<li><a href="ch32.html">32 &middot; 🔎 Retrieval-Augmented Generation & Vector Search</a></li>
<li><a href="ch33.html">33 &middot; 🦾 AI Agents & Tool Use</a></li>
<li><a href="ch34.html">34 &middot; 🌈 Multimodal AI</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🛠️ Applied ML Systems</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch35.html">35 &middot; 🛒 Recommender Systems</a></li>
<li><a href="ch36.html">36 &middot; ⏳ Time Series Analysis & Forecasting</a></li>
<li><a href="ch37.html">37 &middot; 🚨 Anomaly Detection</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">📚 Classical & Symbolic AI</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch38.html">38 &middot; 🧭 Search & Problem Solving</a></li>
<li><a href="ch39.html">39 &middot; 📖 Knowledge Representation & Reasoning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch40.html">40 &middot; 🗺️ Planning, Constraint Satisfaction & Game Playing</a></li>
<li><a href="ch41.html">41 &middot; 🧬 Evolutionary Computation & Metaheuristics</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🚀 Production, Responsibility & Frontier</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch42.html">42 &middot; 🔧 MLOps & LLMOps</a></li>
<li><a href="ch43.html">43 &middot; 🚀 AI Infrastructure & Efficient Inference</a></li>
<li><a href="ch44.html">44 &middot; 🔍 Explainable AI & Interpretability</a></li>
<li><a href="ch45.html">45 &middot; 🧷 Causal Inference & Causal ML</a></li>
<li><a href="ch46.html">46 &middot; ⚖️ AI Ethics, Fairness, Safety & Governance</a></li>
<li><a href="ch47.html">47 &middot; 🌠 Frontier & Emerging Directions</a></li>
</ul>
</details>

------------------------------------------------------------------------

Raw data is almost never ready for a model. It arrives with gaps, typos, wild outliers, text where numbers should be, and columns measured on wildly different scales. **Data preprocessing** is the work of turning that mess into clean, numeric, model-ready features, and **feature engineering** is the craft of constructing inputs that expose the signal a model can actually learn from. In practice this is where most of the time on a real project goes, and where most of the predictive gains (and most of the silent bugs) come from. It sits squarely in the Foundations of the field: every chapter after this assumes your data already arrived in good shape.

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
🧭 **In context:** Foundations of the ML workflow · used to convert raw records into clean numeric features and to prevent train/test leakage · key idea: garbage in, garbage out — the model is only as good as the features you feed it, and every transform must learn its parameters from the training data alone.
:::

## 4.1 — Data cleaning: missing values, outliers, duplicates

Before any clever feature work, the data has to be trustworthy. Cleaning is the unglamorous first pass that handles the three most common defects: values that are missing, values that are extreme, and rows that are repeated. Think of it as wiping the lens before you take the photo — no amount of later processing recovers detail that a dirty lens smeared away.

**Missing values** are entries with no recorded data — a blank salary field, a null timestamp, a `NaN`. They matter for two reasons. First, most algorithms simply cannot compute on a gap: a single `NaN` in a feature vector poisons the dot product. Second, *why* a value is missing often carries information. Statisticians name three regimes, and naming them is not pedantry — the regime decides what you are allowed to do about it.

- **MCAR** (missing completely at random): the gap is unrelated to anything, like a sensor that randomly drops a reading. Dropping or imputing these rows does not bias the sample.
- **MAR** (missing at random): the gap depends on *other observed* columns — older customers skip the "social media handle" field, but you can see their age. You can recover the value from the columns you do have.
- **MNAR** (missing not at random): the gap depends on the *unobserved value itself* — high earners decline to report income, so the missingness is correlated with exactly the number you cannot see.

The distinction drives strategy: MCAR and MAR can often be imputed safely (next section), while MNAR usually demands an explicit "was-missing" indicator because the absence *is* the signal — pretending you can fill it cleanly throws away the most informative thing about the row.

**Outliers** are values far from the bulk of the data. Some are errors (an age of 999, a price of -5); others are genuine but rare (a legitimate billionaire in an income column). The fix depends on which: errors should be corrected or removed, but real extremes are often *kept*, because removing them throws away the very tail you may care about (fraud, failures, and churn all live in the tail). A common, defensible rule for *flagging* candidates is the **IQR fence**: with quartiles $Q_1$ and $Q_3$ and $\text{IQR}=Q_3-Q_1$, flag points outside

$$ [\,Q_1 - 1.5\,\text{IQR},\;\; Q_3 + 1.5\,\text{IQR}\,]. $$

A second common rule is the **z-score** test: flag any point more than, say, $3$ standard deviations from the mean, $|x-\mu|/\sigma > 3$. The IQR fence is the safer default because the mean and standard deviation are themselves dragged around by the very outliers you are hunting, while the median and quartiles are not.

**Duplicates** are repeated rows — often from a join gone wrong or a logging retry. They silently reweight the data: a row appearing twice counts twice in the loss, biasing the model toward it. De-duplicate, but check first whether two "identical" rows are truly the same event or two real events that happen to share values (two genuine $4.99 coffee purchases are not a duplicate).

::: {.callout-warning}
Dropping every row with any missing value (**listwise deletion**) feels clean but is dangerous: if 5% of values are missing independently across 20 columns, you can lose roughly $1-0.95^{20}\approx 64\%$ of your rows — and, worse, you bias the sample if the missingness is MAR/MNAR rather than MCAR.
:::

## 4.2 — Imputation strategies

When you choose to fill gaps rather than drop them, you **impute** — estimate the missing value from what you do know. The simplest approach replaces each missing entry with a column-level constant: the **mean** or **median** for numbers, the **mode** (most frequent category) for categoricals. Median is the safer default for numeric columns because it shrugs off outliers and skew — one billionaire will yank a mean upward but barely move a median.

The intuition for why constant imputation is often "good enough": with no other knowledge, a column's central tendency is the best single guess for a missing entry. But it has a real cost — it shrinks variance and erases correlations, because every gap collapses to the same point, so a scatter plot of an imputed column grows a suspicious vertical stripe. Smarter methods use the *other* features instead of a single constant:

- **KNN imputation** fills a gap with the (weighted) average of the $k$ nearest complete rows, where "near" is measured on the columns that *are* present.
- **Iterative imputation** (the idea behind **MICE**, Multiple Imputation by Chained Equations) models each column with missing data as a regression on the others, then cycles through the columns repeatedly until the filled values stop changing.

Whatever method you pick, pair it with a **missing-indicator** column — a binary flag that is 1 when the original was missing. This lets the model learn from the *fact* of missingness, which is exactly what you need under MNAR, where the gap itself is predictive.

```python
import numpy as np

def median_impute(col):
    # col: 1D array with np.nan for gaps
    med = np.nanmedian(col)                 # median ignores NaNs
    filled = np.where(np.isnan(col), med, col)
    was_missing = np.isnan(col).astype(int) # keep the signal
    return filled, was_missing

x = np.array([2.0, np.nan, 4.0, 100.0, np.nan])
filled, flag = median_impute(x)
assert np.array_equal(flag, [0, 1, 0, 0, 1])
assert filled[1] == np.nanmedian(x)         # filled with 4.0
```

::: {.callout-tip}
Compute the imputation value (the median, the KNN neighbours) on the **training set only**, then apply it to validation and test. Computing it over the whole dataset leaks information from the rows you are about to be tested on — see §4.7.
:::

## 4.3 — Scaling and normalization

Many models care about the *magnitude* of features, not just their pattern. A column of incomes (tens of thousands) and a column of ages (tens) live on incomparable scales; left raw, income will dominate any distance or gradient simply because its numbers are bigger — not because it matters more. **Scaling** rewrites features onto comparable ranges so no feature wins on units alone.

There are three workhorses.

**Standardization** (z-score) centres each column to mean $0$ and scales to unit variance:

$$ z = \frac{x - \mu}{\sigma}. $$

It is the default for most cases. It does not require the data to be bell-shaped, but it is most natural when the column is roughly symmetric.

**Min-max scaling** squeezes values into a fixed range, usually $[0,1]$:

$$ x' = \frac{x - x_{\min}}{x_{\max} - x_{\min}}. $$

Useful when you need bounded inputs (image pixels, or a neural net's input layer), but a single outlier sets $x_{\max}$ and crushes everything else into a sliver near $0$.

**Robust scaling** uses the median and IQR instead of mean and standard deviation:

$$ x' = \frac{x - \text{median}(x)}{\text{IQR}(x)}, $$

so it shrugs off outliers — reach for it when the column has heavy tails.

Which models *need* scaling? Anything that measures distances or sums weighted inputs: k-NN, k-means, SVMs, PCA, and gradient-descent-trained models (linear/logistic regression, neural nets) all converge faster and behave better when features share a scale. Tree-based models (decision trees, random forests, gradient boosting) are **scale-invariant** — they split on thresholds like "income $> 50\text{k}$", and any monotonic rescaling moves the threshold but not the split, so it changes nothing.

| Scaler | Center / spread | Output range | Outlier-robust | When to use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Standardization | mean / std | unbounded, ~mean 0 | no | general default |
| Min-max | min / range | $[0,1]$ | no | bounded inputs, image pixels |
| Robust | median / IQR | unbounded | yes | heavy-tailed columns |

::: {.callout-warning}
"Normalization" is an overloaded word. Here it usually means rescaling a feature's range; in other corners it means scaling each *row* to unit length (L2 normalization), or making a distribution Gaussian (§4.5). When someone says "normalize the data", ask which one they mean before you reach for a formula.
:::

## 4.4 — Encoding categorical variables

Models eat numbers, but much real data is **categorical** — colours, countries, product IDs. Encoding turns categories into numbers without inventing relationships that are not there.

**One-hot encoding** creates one binary column per category: `color=red` becomes `[1,0,0]`. It makes no ordering assumption, which is exactly right for **nominal** (unordered) categories, but it explodes the feature count when a column has many levels — a "high-cardinality" column like zip code can spawn thousands of sparse columns.

**Ordinal encoding** maps categories to integers $0,1,2,\dots$. This is correct *only* when the categories have a true order (`low < medium < high`); applying it to unordered categories tells the model that `green (1)` sits "between" `red (0)` and `blue (2)`, a fiction a distance- or gradient-based model will happily try to exploit.

**Target (mean) encoding** replaces each category with the mean of the target for that category — encode `city` by the average house price in that city. This is compact and powerful for high-cardinality columns, but it is the single biggest leakage trap in preprocessing: if you compute a category's mean using the very row whose target you are predicting, you leak the answer into the feature. It must be done with **out-of-fold** computation (each row encoded using only *other* folds) and **smoothing** toward the global mean so a category seen once does not get a wildly overconfident value.

**Embeddings** learn a dense, low-dimensional vector per category, trained jointly with the model (standard in deep learning and recommenders). They capture similarity — two cities that behave alike end up with nearby vectors — and handle very high cardinality gracefully, since the table size grows with the embedding dimension, not the square of the vocabulary.

```python
import numpy as np

def one_hot(labels, categories):
    idx = {c: i for i, c in enumerate(categories)}
    M = np.zeros((len(labels), len(categories)), dtype=int)
    for r, lab in enumerate(labels):
        M[r, idx[lab]] = 1            # exactly one 1 per row
    return M

cats = ["red", "green", "blue"]
oh = one_hot(["green", "blue", "red"], cats)
assert oh.tolist() == [[0,1,0],[0,0,1],[1,0,0]]
```

::: {.callout-warning}
Categories present at test time but unseen during training (a brand-new city) break naive encoders. Decide the policy up front: reserve an explicit "unknown" bucket, or map rare and unseen levels to the global mean. Discovering this in production is a bad time to decide.
:::

## 4.5 — Skew and transformations

Many real columns are heavily **right-skewed** — incomes, counts, response times — with a long tail of large values. Skew hurts scale-sensitive and linear models: a handful of huge values dominate, residuals fan out, and the assumption of roughly symmetric noise breaks. A transformation that compresses the tail often restores good behaviour.

The everyday tool is the **log transform**, $x \mapsto \log(x)$, which pulls in large values hard and stretches out small ones, so a multiplicative spread (incomes from \$10k to \$10M) becomes an additive one. Because $\log(0)$ is undefined, use $\log(1+x)$ (`log1p`) for non-negative data with zeros. Multiplicative effects become additive under a log, which is exactly why it tames things like prices and populations.

When you want the data made as Gaussian as possible and the exponent chosen for you, use a **power transform**. The **Box-Cox** transform is a family indexed by a parameter $\lambda$, fit to maximize normality:

$$ x^{(\lambda)} = \begin{cases} \dfrac{x^{\lambda} - 1}{\lambda} & \lambda \neq 0 \\[6pt] \ln x & \lambda = 0 \end{cases} $$

Box-Cox requires strictly positive inputs; the **Yeo-Johnson** variant extends the same idea to zero and negative values, so it is the safer default when a column can dip below zero.

<svg viewBox="0 0 360 130" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg">
  <line x1="20" y1="100" x2="170" y2="100" stroke="currentColor"/>
  <path d="M25 100 C 35 30, 45 30, 60 60 C 75 90, 120 98, 165 99" fill="none" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="2"/>
  <text x="55" y="118" font-size="11" fill="currentColor">skewed (raw)</text>
  <text x="180" y="55" font-size="12" fill="currentColor">— log →</text>
  <line x1="200" y1="100" x2="350" y2="100" stroke="currentColor"/>
  <path d="M210 99 C 250 25, 300 25, 345 99" fill="none" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="2"/>
  <text x="235" y="118" font-size="11" fill="currentColor">~symmetric</text>
</svg>

A practical note: if you log-transform the *target* before training, remember to exponentiate predictions back, and know that the mean of logs is not the log of the mean — naive back-transformation slightly under-predicts, a bias worth correcting when the numbers matter.

## 4.6 — Feature engineering and feature selection

**Feature engineering** is constructing new inputs that make the signal easier to learn. A model can only combine what you give it; handing it the right combination up front is often worth more than a fancier algorithm. The classic example: from a raw timestamp a model learns almost nothing, but `is_weekend` derived from it can be the strongest predictor in the table.

Common moves:

- **Interactions:** combine columns the model cannot easily build itself — `price_per_sqft = price / area`, or a flag for `is_weekend AND is_holiday`. Linear models especially cannot multiply two features on their own.
- **Binning:** bucket a continuous column into ranges (`age` → child/adult/senior). This adds robustness to outliers and lets a linear model capture non-linear steps, at the cost of throwing away within-bin detail.
- **Datetime expansion:** a raw timestamp is nearly useless as a single integer; explode it into hour, day-of-week, month, and `is_weekend`, and use **cyclical encodings** $\left(\sin\frac{2\pi h}{24},\, \cos\frac{2\pi h}{24}\right)$ so that hour 23 sits right next to hour 0 instead of $23$ units away.
- **Text and image features:** counts, TF-IDF, or learned embeddings for text; edges, histograms, or CNN activations for images (the heavy machinery lives in the NLP and Computer Vision chapters).

Once you have many features, **feature selection** keeps the useful ones and drops the rest, which reduces overfitting, speeds training, and aids interpretability. The three families differ in *how much they involve the model*:

- **Filter** methods score each feature independently of any model (correlation with the target, mutual information, a variance threshold). Fast, but blind to interactions between features.
- **Wrapper** methods train a model on subsets of features and search for the best-scoring set (for example, recursive feature elimination). Accurate but expensive, since each candidate set means another training run.
- **Embedded** methods do selection *during* training — L1/Lasso regularization drives some weights exactly to zero, and tree ensembles expose feature importances you can threshold.

Distinguish selection from **feature extraction**, which *creates* new compressed features (as in PCA) out of combinations of the originals rather than choosing among existing ones. Extraction is covered in the Dimensionality Reduction chapter.

```{mermaid}
flowchart TD
  A[Many candidate features] --> B{How involved<br/>is the model?}
  B -->|not at all| C[Filter<br/>correlation, MI, variance]
  B -->|fully, on subsets| D[Wrapper<br/>recursive elimination]
  B -->|during training| E[Embedded<br/>Lasso, tree importance]
  C --> F[Selected feature set]
  D --> F
  E --> F
```

::: {.callout-tip}
Reach for more and better features before you reach for a fancier model. A plain linear model on well-engineered inputs routinely beats a deep network fed raw columns — and it trains in seconds and is far easier to debug.
:::

## 4.7 — Data leakage and pipelines

The most damaging preprocessing bug is silent: **data leakage**, where information from outside the training set sneaks into the features, so the model looks brilliant in validation and then falls apart in production. The classic culprit is fitting a transform on *all* the data. If you compute the mean for standardization, or the median for imputation, over the full dataset and *then* split, your training features already encode facts about the test rows — and your validation score is quietly inflated.

The rule is simple and absolute: **fit transforms on the training fold only, then apply (transform) to validation and test.** *Fit* learns parameters ($\mu$, $\sigma$, category means, the Box-Cox $\lambda$); *transform* applies already-learned parameters without looking at new targets.

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
  A[Raw data] --> B[Split first]
  B --> C[Train fold]
  B --> D[Test fold]
  C -->|fit + transform| E[Fitted transforms<br/>μ, σ, medians, λ]
  E -->|transform only| D
  C --> F[Train model]
  E -.params.-> F
```

This is exactly what a **pipeline** enforces. A pipeline chains the preprocessing steps and the final model into one object whose `fit` runs only on training data and whose `transform`/`predict` reuse those learned parameters. Beyond tidiness, it makes cross-validation correct — each fold re-fits the transforms on just its own training portion, so leakage cannot creep in through the back door — and it ships the identical transformation logic to production, eliminating the **train/serve skew** where preprocessing code silently diverges between the notebook and the deployed service.

::: {.callout-warning}
Leakage also hides in the *features themselves*: a column that is a proxy for the target (such as "was the loan repaid" included when predicting default), or a future-dated value sitting in a time-series row. Splitting correctly does not save you from a feature that simply should not be available at prediction time — when a single feature looks too good to be true, suspect leakage first.
:::

## 4.x — Key takeaways

- Cleaning comes first: handle missing values (knowing whether they are MCAR / MAR / MNAR), separate real extremes from error outliers, and remove true duplicates before anything else.
- Impute with median or mode as a baseline, always add a missing-indicator flag, and reach for KNN or iterative (MICE) imputation when columns are correlated.
- Scale features (standardization, min-max, or robust) for any distance- or gradient-based model; tree models are scale-invariant and need none of it.
- Encode categoricals by their nature: one-hot for nominal, ordinal only for truly ordered, target encoding for high cardinality (with out-of-fold computation and smoothing), embeddings for deep models.
- Transform skewed columns with log / `log1p` or Box-Cox / Yeo-Johnson to help linear and scale-sensitive models.
- Engineer features (interactions, binning, datetime, cyclical) and select them via filter / wrapper / embedded methods — selection chooses among existing features, extraction creates new ones.
- Prevent leakage by fitting every transform on the training fold only, and wrap the whole flow in a pipeline so cross-validation and production stay honest.

## 4.y — See also

- **Chapter 03 — The Machine Learning Process** (where preprocessing sits in the end-to-end workflow)
- **Chapter 11 — Model Evaluation, Validation & Selection** (cross-validation, the setting where leakage bites hardest)
- **Chapter 10 — Dimensionality Reduction & Manifold Learning** (feature extraction: PCA and beyond)
- **Chapter 02 — Mathematics for Machine Learning** (the statistics behind means, variance, and distributions)
- **Chapter 28 — Natural Language Processing** and **Chapter 27 — Computer Vision** (deep text and image feature extraction)
- **Chapter 36 — Time Series Analysis & Forecasting** (temporal features and the strict no-future-leakage rule)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[📖 All chapters](../ai-ml-encyclopedia.html)  &nbsp;|&nbsp;  [← 03 · 🔄 The Machine Learning Process](ch03.html)  &nbsp;|&nbsp;  [05 · 📈 Linear & Logistic Models →](ch05.html)
